76: **Brazil**, level with one other team on 7 points after three group games, open the day's action at 10:00 AM PT against **Japan**, who finished 2nd in **Group F** on 5 points. **Brazil**'s goal-difference edge of +6 underlines their attacking threat, though **Japan**'s own +4 suggests this is no pushover — the Asian side have the tactical discipline and set-piece quality to test any defence. A South American giant versus a resurgent Asian outfit: the Round of 32 starts with a statement.
74: **Germany**, level with one other team on 6 points heading into the knockouts, meet **Paraguay**, also unable to be separated from a fellow side after finishing level on 4 points. Both teams have questions to answer: **Germany** must prove their point-share doesn't mask defensive vulnerabilities, while **Paraguay** — negative on goal difference — need to show they can boss a game rather than merely survive one. A tight, tactical affair looks inevitable at 1:30 PM PT.
75: The evening showpiece at 6:00 PM PT sends **Netherlands**, the outright winners of **Group F** on 7 points, against a **Morocco** side that is level with one other team on 7 points, their exact position in their own group not yet resolved. Both arrive with identical points hauls but contrasting narratives — **Netherlands** dominant going forward, **Morocco** built on defensive solidity and lethal transitions. This one has the quality and the stakes to define the entire round.
73: History beckons for both sides: **South Africa**, sitting 2nd in **Group A** on 4 points, face **Canada**, who are level with one other team on 4 points, their position in their own group not yet separated — making this a genuine meeting of equals before a ball is kicked. Neither nation has ever won a knockout tie at this stage of the finals, and that changes today for one of them. With Alphonso Davies fit for **Canada** and Teboho Mokoena returning for **South Africa**, the midfield battle could settle it — and extra time and penalties are waiting if 90 minutes aren't enough.
